text: Hello viewer, my name is Dr. Vishal Soni and today we will be watching a case of robotic hernia surgery and with that I give you greetings from the hernia clinic at Zydus Hospitals, Ahmedabad. We will be using anatomical landmarks to identify the incision and placement of various steps in a robotic hernia surgery.

The video shows the capillary network on the peritoneum moving laterally with respiration. A dotted line indicates the location of the linea semilunaris. The transversus abdominis region moves with respiration, while the peritoneum remains densely adhered below the rectus abdominis, marking the linea semilunaris.

We now move to how to identify whether a tap can be performed or a term would be needed. It's a simple pinch test that needs to be done at the level of the umbilicus about five to seven centimeters proximally and five to seven centimeters distally. The moment the peritoneum is pinchable, the tap is doable.
The surgeon begins placing the peritoneal incision for a ventral tap meshplasty, a procedure addressing a ventral or umbilical hernia.

The surgeon identifies the linea semilunaris on the opposite side using anatomical landmarks, then performs a pinch test on the peritoneum to assess its separability, confirming the feasibility of the tap procedure.

The surgeon makes an incision along the border of the linea semilunaris, positioned medially, extending approximately seven to ten centimeters above and below the umbilicus. This incision defines the upper limit for mesh accommodation in the procedure.

The surgeon encounters loose and flimsy areolar tissue in the surgical plane, which separates easily with swiping movements. In this patient, there is also some fleshy areolar tissue with sizable vessels that require an energy source for division.
The surgeon focuses on the distal end of the incision, moving lower to extend the incision further, preparing to create the necessary space for the procedure.
Thank you for watching. Have a great day.
